item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. However as a parent, I'm forced to ask; what became of the monkey?

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and therefore is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm not a big fan of this one. The concept isn't that interesting to me and there are a few areas of the article I felt were off. The redaction regarding the temporal lobe activity didn't really give enough info to allow the reader to form their own opinion on what happened. Same with the dolphin test. My final gripe (and I freely admit my ignorance here) is regarding the first test. Does a lack of formal education preclude aptitude in a subject? The vagrant had no formal education but unlike the other tests this one just assumed that the individual was poor at maths. There are a number of very intelligent people who have been made homeless due to mental health issue or addictions. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree.
I feel that 1248 is not reciprocal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The expunged dolphin test irritates me somewhat.
I feel as if I don't have enough information to come to any conclusion.
The rest of it reads well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the heck happened with the dolphin?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last test… well, he deserved it. It's nice to finally see bad things happen to bad people, it usually is the other way around and it leaves you feeling sad or sorry. Not this time, I just kept imagining some kind of rapist Hannibal Lecter seeing his intelligence wither away progresively until nothing remained, and how terrible it must have been. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the idea of switching intelligence with someone, and not knowing what you lost, to be a rather terrifying concept. Also, the last test is especially scary to me. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you mean about the third point. The idea is that there is no possible way to arrange the blocks such that the pattern can be replicated, even if there were no time limit. It originally read differently but I changed it to make it clear that there was a time limit and that therefore the task could be completed, even if the only possible result was failure. Will have to think of a better way to phrase it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some minor quibbles:
"…approximately 8 seconds…" - That is a very precise aproximation.
"…nonstandard spellings." - Speaking of which, isn't it non-standard?
"…impossible to achieve with the blocks provided within the allotted time,…" - If all the blocks are there, then the pattern is not impossible. It may be extremely unlikely that someone could meet the time limit, but with the data provided it would be impossible to categorically prove the task "impossible".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It could go either way here. On one hand, there's not much there, and that redaction that the temporal lobe activity is consistent with is a bit obscure. Also, it isn't made clear that the testtaker and the administrator SWAP aptitudes; I at first thought that the administrator's aptitude was COPIED into the testtaker's. You might want to clarify that a little bit.
Still, enough for a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After seeing so many incorrect usages of IQ, I am BEYOND glad to finally see someone approach intelligence/IQ correctly. So congratulations to you on that. :-)

That said, I find the core concept (switching aptitudes) kinda dull, but you handle it well, so I'm borderline. No vote for now, may come back and upvote later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. I especially like all the allusions to alchemy. The Foundation has no idea how it's relevant, but it clearly is relevant… somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing involving a macaque trained to proclaim its ignorance can be anything less than worthwhile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is kind of a dumb idea, but it's well written and the part with the monkey was just so funny that I had to upvote this. Well done sir.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… it reminds me too much of SCP-1248.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. The last test caused me to change my vote from no vote to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback, all - I did take this to chat to make sure it was mostly OK, but some time in the drafts forum would probably have done it some good.

Made some changes for clarity and unlolfoundationness, and added another test at the end that I was thinking of doing anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted. The tone is good, the tests, though some were a bit humorous, were not lolfoundation. The concept isn't horribly original and the article itself kinda just sits there, but it is functional enough that I feel like it should have its rating leveled out slightly. The

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I thought the mind-swapping thing was a bit boring in itself. But the dolphin experiment suggest that it can have interesting and scary results. I think that aspect could be more prominent. That reminded me of the dolphins from the Hitchiker's guide. (And I really liked the monkey with the “I don't know” sign). I don't really see it as lolfoundation. The tests made me laugh, but the researchers are not trying to goof off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like there must be several more interesting effects that this could have instead of/in addition to swapping knowledge. Also I have to downvote due to lolfoundation, I would definitely recommend losing those parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Within 500 milliseconds of task completion…

SCP 1327-10 (block design task): The design which test-takers are meant to duplicate is impossible to achieve with the blocks provided.

So this test is safe, aside from the risk of the test-giver winding up with a design-block enema…

…temporal lobe activation consistent with █████████.

*gasp* Not █████████! My third cousin twice removed was killed by a tractor while trying to █████████!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd upvote this it if wasn't for the fact that they tested a mindwiping item on a member of staff

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Hell. This, to me, looks like pretty damn good writing, style and tone-wise that I really, really don't want to downvote. However, as said upthread, you don't do much with the concept. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like Esko said, this isn't the most interesting idea, but it was executed well. I'm going to give you the benefit of the doubt with this one. So, a +1. I don't know if you did this or not, but take your new drafts, when you have any, to the Chat or the forums. People can look over the drafts and help you with problems it may have. And again, I don't know if you took this SCP to the chat, but next time, I'd advise against cold posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, execution is pretty good, but the idea itself (IQ test what makes tester and testee SWITCH BRAAAAAAAINS) is where the issue is. Also, you include such discouraged Old-School SCP Throwbacks as the ever-beloved humorous researcher note. Also your SCP has a shrugging dolphin. Your SCP has a shrugging dolphin. If it weren't for the principle of the thing, I'd upvote for audacity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting idea, but unfortunately I don't think you've really done anything with it.
